meta.title: Nerve Pain (Trigeminal Neuralgia) | Vincor
meta.description: Suffering from facial pain or trigeminal neuralgia? Discover how jaw dysfunction can cause nerve pain. Vincor offers advanced 4D-diagnosis and treatment.

hero.title: Nerve Pain & Trigeminal Neuralgia
hero.description: Sharp, stabbing facial pain can indicate trigeminal neuralgia or referred pain from the jaw joint. At Vincor, we investigate the role of the jaw in chronic facial nerve pain.

watIsHet.label: What is it
watIsHet.title: What is trigeminal neuralgia?
paragraphs: The nervus trigeminus (trigeminal nerve) is the fifth cranial nerve and the main sensory nerve of the face. This nerve branches into three main divisions: the ophthalmic division (V1), the maxillary division (V2), and the mandibular division (V3). Trigeminal neuralgia is a condition where this nerve becomes intensely painful in paroxysmal attacks.

The pain is often described as an electric shock, a burning sensation, or a sharp stab in the face. Attacks can be triggered by everyday activities such as speaking, chewing, brushing teeth, or even a gust of wind. What many patients and doctors don't know is that the mandibular division (V3) also innervates the jaw joint and chewing muscles, making jaw dysfunction an important source of trigeminal pain complaints.

symptomen.title: Do you recognize these symptoms?
symptomen.description: Nerve pain in the face can manifest in many ways. The following symptoms may indicate a jaw-related component.
items: Sharp, stabbing or burning pain in the cheek, jaw, or temple
Paroxysmal pain lasting seconds to minutes
Pain triggered by chewing, speaking, or touching
One-sided facial pain incorrectly diagnosed as toothache
Numbness or tingling in the face or lips
Dull, continuous pain alternating with sharp attacks
Pain that worsens with jaw movements or pressure on the jaw joint
Avoidance behavior: no longer daring to chew on the painful side

oorzaken.label: Causes
oorzaken.title: The connection between jaw and nerve pain
paragraphs: The nervus trigeminus plays a dual role: it manages both the sensory perception of the face and the control of the chewing muscles. When the jaw joint becomes dysfunctional or the chewing muscles become chronically overloaded, this can stimulate and sensitize the trigeminal nerve.

With a disturbed occlusion, the chewing muscles are loaded asymmetrically. The resulting trigger points in the musculus pterygoideus lateralis and medialis can refer pain to areas innervated by the trigeminal nerve. This phenomenon is called "referred pain" and explains why patients experience pain in the cheek, ear, or even eye while the source lies in the jaw joint.

Additionally, a displaced articular disc in the jaw joint can exert direct pressure on the auriculotemporal nerve — a branch of the trigeminal nerve — which runs directly behind the jaw joint. Correction of the jaw position removes this pressure and can significantly reduce nerve stimulation.

diagnostiek.title: How we investigate nerve pain
diagnostiek.description: Our 4D-diagnosis accurately identifies whether and how your jaw function contributes to trigeminal pain complaints.

cards.title: 3D joint scan
cards.description: Detailed imaging of the jaw joint to assess compression of nerve structures.

cards.title: Trigger point mapping
cards.description: Systematic palpation and EMG analysis to locate myofascial trigger points that cause referred pain.

cards.title: Diagnostic block
cards.description: Targeted anesthesia test to confirm that the jaw joint region is the source of the referred pain.

behandeling.title: Treatment options for nerve pain
behandeling.description: When the jaw joint is involved in your nerve pain, targeted treatment of the bite and jaw position can provide significant relief.

phases.title: Pain Relief
phases.description: Direct reduction of nerve stimulation by decompressing the jaw joint.
features: Trigger point deactivation of chewing muscles

phases.title: Position Correction
phases.description: Structural restoration of jaw position to relieve nerve compression.

phases.title: Stabilization
phases.description: Long-term maintenance of pain relief and prevention of renewed nerve stimulation.

items.question: What is the difference between trigeminal neuralgia and jaw pain?
items.answer: Classic trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by sudden, severe, electric shock-like pain bursts lasting seconds. Jaw pain (CMD) is typically a more continuous, dull or squeezing pain. In practice, these conditions often overlap: jaw dysfunction can stimulate the trigeminal nerve and thus trigger nerve pain attacks. Our diagnosis makes the distinction and identifies the precise contribution of the jaw joint.

items.question: I already use medication for my nerve pain. Can Vincor still help?
items.answer: Absolutely. Our treatment is complementary to medication therapy. When the jaw is a contributing factor, removing this source can reduce nerve stimulation. Many patients notice that after treatment they need less medication. Changes in medication should always be made in consultation with your treating neurologist or general practitioner.

items.question: How can a bite problem cause nerve pain?
items.answer: The trigeminal nerve manages both the sensation in the face and the control of the chewing muscles. An incorrect bite leads to chronic overloading of these muscles, creating trigger points that refer pain via the trigeminal nerve pathways. Additionally, a displaced articular disc in the jaw joint can exert direct pressure on a branch of the trigeminal nerve. Both mechanisms are scientifically documented.

items.question: Is the treatment painful?
items.answer: No, our treatment is aimed at relieving pain, not causing it. The diagnostic phase is non-invasive and pain-free. The splint therapy and neuromuscular orthotic are comfortable and gradually adjusted. Many patients experience relief already from the first wearing of the splint because the pressure on the jaw joint and nerve structures decreases.
